scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Meta-prompting. [pause] Thinking through a task — with AI — before you ask for the answer.

scenes.name: When
scenes.text: Sometimes you know the problem but not the solution. [pause] <emphasis>My students struggle with multiplication tables, but I'm not sure what kind of exercise works best.</emphasis> [pause] Writing a precise prompt feels premature.

scenes.name: Definition
scenes.text: <emphasis>Meta-prompting</emphasis> is prompting about prompting. [pause] You don't say <emphasis>make me X</emphasis>. You say <emphasis>I'm trying to achieve Y — what are my options?</emphasis>

scenes.name: Reflection
scenes.text: It aligns perfectly with reflective practice. [pause] Teachers already ask — what do I want to achieve? What approach suits this group? [pause] Meta-prompting extends that conversation to an AI partner.

scenes.name: Version 1
scenes.text: Meta-prompting <emphasis>1.0</emphasis> — improving an existing prompt. [pause] You write a draft. You ask the AI: <emphasis>how could I improve this for a more precise result?</emphasis> [pause] It suggests what is missing. You rewrite.

scenes.name: Version 2
scenes.text: Meta-prompting <emphasis>2.0</emphasis> — exploring options. [pause] You start with no prompt. Just a problem. [pause] AI lays out approaches. You pick one. Then — and only then — you write the actual prompt.

scenes.name: Cycle
scenes.text: Four moves in a cycle. [pause] Explore — discuss the options. Decide — pick the approach. Prompt — write the specific request. Refine — iterate the output.

scenes.name: When use
scenes.text: When to use it. [pause] When you know the problem, not the solution. When the task is complex. When you want to compare approaches. [pause] For simple tasks — direct prompting is enough.
